lesson.title: Matrix Inverse
lesson.body: The inverse of a square matrix A is the unique matrix Aâپ»آ¹ such that AAâپ»آ¹ = Aâپ»آ¹A = I. An inverse exists if and only if det(A) â‰  0. For 2أ—2 matrices: Aâپ»آ¹ = (1/det(A))آ·[[d, -b], [-c, a]]. For larger matrices, the Gauss-Jordan method augments [A|I] and row-reduces to [I|Aâپ»آ¹]. Key property: (AB)âپ»آ¹ = Bâپ»آ¹Aâپ»آ¹.

rooms.question: Which property is true for invertible matrices A and B?
rooms.explanation: (AB)âپ»آ¹ = Bâپ»آ¹Aâپ»آ¹ â€” note the order reversal. This is called the 'socks and shoes' property.

rooms.question: To solve Ax = b using the inverse, we write:
rooms.explanation: If A is invertible, multiply both sides of Ax=b on the left by Aâپ»آ¹ to get x = Aâپ»آ¹b.

rooms.question: Order the Gauss-Jordan steps to find Aâپ»آ¹:
items: Apply row operations to reduce A to I
Verify the left block is I
The right block is now Aâپ»آ¹
rooms.explanation: Gauss-Jordan: augment with identity, row-reduce until identity is on the left, and the right side becomes the inverse.

rooms.question: If A is an nأ—n matrix with det(A) = 0, then:
rooms.explanation: A singular matrix (det = 0) is not invertible. The system Ax = b either has no solution or infinitely many.

boss.name: The Non-Invertible
boss.taunt: I am singular. Your operations end here.

questions.question: If Aآ² = A (A is idempotent) and A is invertible, then:
questions.explanation: If Aآ² = A, multiply both sides by Aâپ»آ¹: A = I. The only invertible idempotent matrix is the identity.

questions.question: (Aلµ€)âپ»آ¹ equals:
questions.explanation: (Aلµ€)âپ»آ¹ = (Aâپ»آ¹)لµ€. Transpose and inverse commute in this sense.

questions.question: The adjugate (classical adjoint) formula gives Aâپ»آ¹ = (1/det(A))آ·adj(A). For a 2أ—2 matrix [[a,b],[c,d]], adj(A) is:
questions.explanation: The adjugate swaps the diagonal and negates the off-diagonal entries of a 2أ—2 matrix.
